creator: stas@FreeBSD.org
text:
On Mon, 11 Oct 2010 10:45:43 +0100
Pete French <petefrench@ticketswitch.com> mentioned:

> If there are good reasons that this port needs to behave differently to
> other library ports then I understand, but could it be added as an option
> at least ?
> 

Actually, this is not the only port that doesn't install a static library.
I'd say the only reason a lot of ports install them is that they're using
libtool which is hard to prevent installing static libraries along with
infamous .la files.

The reason we do it is exactly to prevent static linking to happen.  Imagine
the pretty common situation when some kind of security vulnerability is
discovered in libdispatch: in that case we'll fix the vulnerability and
document it in vuxml database, so the user will be informed that this
happened and he will update the affected port (either by hand or automatically).
If some other ports were dependent on this one, they won't need to be rebuild,
they will just pick up the updated library and the vulnerability will
be fixed in them as well.  Buf if any of them were linked to this library
statically, there's no way to tell that this particular application is
vulnerable too, neither there's a way to automatically update it.

Something similar happens when bugs gets fixed in the library port:
in the case when all dependent applications were linked dynamically,
we'll just fix the bug in the library and it will be fixed in all
applications simultaneously.  Otherwise, the bug will be fixed only
in some subset of them.

I understand, that static linking might be desirable for some
commercial applications.  One of solutions I could suggest is
to install the static version of the library in some special
folder when you will be able to link agains it by providing
the path to it explicitly.  This way we'll be able to prevent
accidential static linking from happening.

-- 
Stanislav Sedov
ST4096-RIPE
